title: On the Chow motive of some hyperKaehler varieties
speaker: Charles Vial
tags: Workshop on Chow Groups Motives and Derived Categories

proof_stages.label: Beauville Fourier splitting for abelian varieties
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the abelian-variety model: the Poincaré bundle defines Beauville's Fourier transform on Chow groups, giving a canonical bigrading with eigenspace, multiplicativity, and explicit projector properties.

proof_stages.label: Chow–Künneth and expected Bloch–Beilinson framework
proof_stages.summary: The abelian projectors are reinterpreted as a Chow–Künneth decomposition, leading to the general conjectural filtration on Chow groups and the question of when this filtration is multiplicatively split.

proof_stages.label: Beauville–Voisin weak splitting for K3 surfaces
proof_stages.summary: For a K3 surface, Beauville–Voisin's canonical zero-cycle makes intersections of divisors and c2 rationally controlled, motivating the weak splitting property for hyperkähler varieties and displaying both known cases and failures.

proof_stages.label: Fourier reinterpretation of the K3 theorem
proof_stages.summary: Using the standard K3 Chow–Künneth projectors, the Beauville–Voisin theorem is recast as a Fourier-type decomposition whose middle projector is the algebraic inverse of the intersection form on H2.

proof_stages.label: Fano variety of lines and Voisin incidence identity
proof_stages.summary: The main target is set as the Fano variety F(X) of lines on a smooth cubic fourfold, with tautological classes and the incidence correspondence I satisfying Voisin's quadratic identity, suggesting a Fourier kernel.

proof_stages.label: Canonical Beauville–Bogomolov cycle L
proof_stages.summary: Because the incidence class is not deformation-invariant, it is replaced by a Chow class L whose cohomology is the inverse Beauville–Bogomolov form and which satisfies a quadratic relation lifting the cohomological one.

proof_stages.label: Fourier decomposition for Fano varieties of lines
proof_stages.summary: The Fourier transform with kernel L produces a Chow-group decomposition and Chow–Künneth projectors, with multiplicativity for very general cubic fourfolds proved through explicit identities and geometric input such as Voisin's rational map.

proof_stages.label: Multiplicative Chow–Künneth decompositions and stability
proof_stages.summary: The stronger Beauville–Voisin modified-diagonal statement is formulated as multiplicativity of a Chow–Künneth decomposition, and a final theorem asserts existence and stability of such decompositions under products, projectivized tangent bundles, and Hilbert schemes of length two and three in several classes of varieties.

examples.name: General versus hyperelliptic curves for multiplicativity
examples.note: Curves show that a split Bloch–Beilinson filtration is much weaker than a multiplicative Chow–Künneth decomposition, since the latter fails for general curves of genus greater than three but holds in the hyperelliptic case.
examples.engagement_reason: the general-versus-hyperelliptic curve contrast is stated as known behavior of multiplicativity, without carrying out the modified-diagonal computation

passes.reason: General curves of genus greater than three serve as failures of multiplicativity, while the hyperelliptic comparison lies at minimal gonality.
General genus >3 curves give failures of multiplicative CK, while hyperelliptic curves are the minimal-gonality positive boundary case.
General curves of genus greater than three show split filtration need not give a multiplicative Chow-Künneth decomposition, contrasted with hyperelliptic curves.
General curves of genus greater than three are used as failures of multiplicative Chow–Künneth decompositions despite split filtrations, with hyperelliptic curves as the positive contrast.
Hyperelliptic curves form the minimum-gonality curve locus, while general genus-greater-than-three curves are used to show multiplicative CK decompositions can fail.
General curves of genus greater than three are used to show that a split filtration need not come from a multiplicative Chow–Künneth decomposition, with hyperelliptic curves as the positive contrast.

General curves of genus greater than three show that a split filtration does not imply a multiplicative CK decomposition, while the hyperelliptic comparison has its characteristic involutive symmetry.
